plugin_desc: NetApp storage-specific SNMP collection for NetApp FAS/AFF/ONTAP storage systems (NETWORK-APPLIANCE-MIB, IANA PEN 789). In addition to standard IF-MIB interface metrics, it collects NetApp private OIDs for device health — controller CPU busy time as a percentage (cpuBusyTimePerCent) and the per-RAID disk-drive status (raidStatus, one row per drive). The RAID raw codes are normalized via telegraf processors.enum to 1=healthy / 2=fault and the worst drive is taken per device with max(). Supported brands/models — NetApp FAS, AFF and ONTAP-based storage systems with SNMP enabled.
name: Storage

metrics.name: device_cpu_usage
metrics.description: This metric reports the controller CPU busy time of the NetApp storage system (NETWORK-APPLIANCE-MIB cpuBusyTimePerCent), expressed as a percentage from 0 to 100. Sustained high CPU usage can indicate controller overload, heavy IO or deduplication/replication workload and may slow down host IO response.

metrics.name: device_disk_state
metrics.description: This metric reports the physical disk-drive health of the NetApp storage system, derived from the per-RAID drive status table (NETWORK-APPLIANCE-MIB raidStatus, one row per drive). The raw codes (active(1) is healthy; reconstructing/failed and other non-active states are faults) are normalized via telegraf processors.enum so that 1 = healthy and 2 = fault, and the worst drive is taken per device with max(). A value greater than 1 indicates a degraded or failed drive that risks data loss and warrants immediate replacement.

metrics.name: interface_ifHCInOctets
metrics.description: This metric indicates the average rate of bytes received over the past 5 minutes, measured in bytes per second, from the 64-bit IF-MIB ifHCInOctets counter (ifXTable). The 64-bit counter avoids the rapid wrap of the 32-bit ifInOctets on high-speed storage uplinks, keeping the rate accurate.

metrics.name: interface_ifHCOutOctets
metrics.description: This metric calculates the average rate of bytes sent over the past 5 minutes, measured in bytes per second, from the 64-bit IF-MIB ifHCOutOctets counter (ifXTable). The 64-bit counter avoids the rapid wrap of the 32-bit ifOutOctets on high-speed storage uplinks, keeping the rate accurate.
